title: INNER JOIN

overview.what:
INNER JOIN combines rows from two tables where a condition is true — typically matching a foreign key in one table to a primary key in another. Rows that have no match in the other table are silently dropped from the result.

Think of it as a filter across two tables: start with every possible pair of rows (the Cartesian product), then keep only the pairs where the ON condition is true.

Against the shared dataset:

  SELECT c.name, o.id AS order_id
  FROM customers c
  INNER JOIN orders o ON o.customer_id = c.id
  ORDER BY c.name, o.id;

This returns 8 rows — one for every order matched to its customer. Customers Greg and Hana appear nowhere because they have no orders; the join silently excludes them.

Mental model: INNER JOIN = 'give me only the rows that exist in BOTH tables'.
overview.why: INNER JOIN is the most common join type. Almost every multi-table query starts here — listing orders with customer names, showing line items with product details, connecting any two related tables. Understanding that unmatched rows vanish is the key insight that separates it from outer joins.
where_used: order detail pages joining orders to products
analytics dashboards combining customers and orders

why_learning_this: Querying across two or more tables is the single most common SQL task — you cannot build any realistic report without joins
Understanding which rows INNER JOIN drops prepares you to choose LEFT JOIN when you need to keep unmatched rows

common_mistakes.title: Forgetting the ON clause
common_mistakes.explanation: Omitting ON (or writing JOIN without a condition) produces a Cartesian product — every row paired with every other row. For 8 customers × 8 orders that's 64 rows instead of 8. Always specify the join condition.

common_mistakes.title: Joining on the wrong column
common_mistakes.explanation: Writing ON c.id = o.id instead of ON c.id = o.customer_id joins customer IDs to order IDs, which happen to match by coincidence in small datasets but produces completely wrong results. Check which column is the foreign key.

common_mistakes.title: Not realizing INNER JOIN drops unmatched rows
common_mistakes.explanation: If a customer has no orders, INNER JOIN silently removes them. This is the correct behavior when you want 'only customers who ordered', but a bug when you want 'all customers, with their orders if any' — use LEFT JOIN for that.

recall_questions.q: What happens to a row in table A when it has no matching row in table B during an INNER JOIN?
recall_questions.answer: It is excluded from the result entirely — INNER JOIN only returns rows that match in both tables.

recall_questions.q: What is the result of an INNER JOIN without an ON clause?
recall_questions.answer: A Cartesian product — every row from the left table paired with every row from the right table.

recall_questions.q: Is 'JOIN' the same as 'INNER JOIN' in PostgreSQL?
recall_questions.answer: Yes — JOIN without a qualifier defaults to INNER JOIN.

understanding_checks.query:
SELECT c.name, o.status
FROM customers c
INNER JOIN orders o ON o.customer_id = c.id
WHERE o.status = 'paid'
ORDER BY c.name;
understanding_checks.question: Which customer names and statuses does this return?
understanding_checks.answer:
Alice, paid
Alice, paid
Bob, paid
Carlos, paid
Emma, paid
Farah, paid
understanding_checks.why: There are 8 orders total; INNER JOIN matches all 8 to their customers. The WHERE clause then keeps only the 6 with status = 'paid', dropping Bob's pending order (order 3) and Diana's cancelled order (order 5).

understanding_checks.query:
SELECT c.name, COUNT(*) AS order_count
FROM customers c
INNER JOIN orders o ON o.customer_id = c.id
GROUP BY c.name
ORDER BY order_count DESC, c.name;
understanding_checks.question: What names and counts appear? Do Greg and Hana appear?
understanding_checks.answer:
Alice, 2
Bob, 2
Carlos, 1
Diana, 1
Emma, 1
Farah, 1

Greg and Hana do NOT appear.
understanding_checks.why: INNER JOIN drops customers with no matching orders. Greg and Hana have zero orders, so they never enter the joined row set and cannot be counted. To include them with a count of 0 you would need a LEFT JOIN with COUNT(o.id).

query_walkthrough.query:
SELECT c.name, p.name AS product, oi.quantity
FROM customers c
INNER JOIN orders o ON o.customer_id = c.id
INNER JOIN order_items oi ON oi.order_id = o.id
INNER JOIN products p ON p.id = oi.product_id
WHERE c.name = 'Alice'
ORDER BY o.id, p.name;
query_walkthrough.focus: Watch four tables chain together: customers → orders → order_items → products. Each INNER JOIN narrows the result to only matching rows, and the WHERE further filters to Alice's rows only.

practice_tasks.title: Add product prices to the order items
practice_tasks.prompt: This query lists order items with their product names. Modify it to also show each product's price by adding the missing column from the products table. Keep the existing JOIN and ordering.
practice_tasks.starter_code:
SELECT oi.id, p.name AS product
FROM order_items oi
INNER JOIN products p ON p.id = oi.product_id
ORDER BY oi.id;
practice_tasks.solution:
SELECT oi.id, p.name AS product, p.price
FROM order_items oi
INNER JOIN products p ON p.id = oi.product_id
ORDER BY oi.id;

challenge.title: Total spend per customer
challenge.prompt: Write a query that shows each customer's name and their total spend (quantity × price across all their order items). Only include customers who have orders. Order by total spend descending, then by name ascending.
challenge.solution:
SELECT c.name, SUM(oi.quantity * p.price) AS total_spend
FROM customers c
INNER JOIN orders o ON o.customer_id = c.id
INNER JOIN order_items oi ON oi.order_id = o.id
INNER JOIN products p ON p.id = oi.product_id
GROUP BY c.name
ORDER BY total_spend DESC, c.name;
